In Java, the code would be:

public int numberOfStableArrays(int zero, int one, int limit) {
    int MOD = 1000000007;
    int[][][] dp0 = new int[zero + 1][one + 1][limit + 1];
    int[][][] dp1 = new int[zero + 1][one + 1][limit + 1];

    // Initialize base cases
    if (zero >= 1) {
        dp0[1][0][1] = 1;
    }
    if (one >= 1) {
        dp1[0][1][1] = 1;
    }

    for (int z = 0; z <= zero; z++) {
        for (int o = 0; o <= one; o++) {
            for (int run = 1; run <= limit; run++) {
                // Process transitions from dp0[z][o][run]
                if (dp0[z][o][run] > 0) {
                    // Add another 0 if possible
                    if (z + 1 <= zero && run + 1 <= limit) {
                        dp0[z + 1][o][run + 1] = (dp0[z + 1][o][run + 1] + dp0[z][o][run]) % MOD;
                    }
                    // Add 1
                    if (o + 1 <= one) {
                        dp1[z][o + 1][1] = (dp1[z][o + 1][1] + dp0[z][o][run]) % MOD;
                    }
                }
                // Process transitions from dp1[z][o][run]
                if (dp1[z][o][run] > 0) {
                    // Add another 1
                    if (o + 1 <= one && run + 1 <= limit) {
                        dp1[z][o + 1][run + 1] = (dp1[z][o + 1][run + 1] + dp1[z][o][run]) % MOD;
                    }
                    // Add 0
                    if (z + 1 <= zero) {
                        dp0[z + 1][o][1] = (dp0[z + 1][o][1] + dp1[z][o][run]) % MOD;
                    }
                }
            }
        }
    }

    long total = 0;
    for (int run = 1; run <= limit; run++) {
        total = (total + dp0[zero][one][run]) % MOD;
        total = (total + dp1[zero][one][run]) % MOD;
    }
    return (int) total;
}

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public int numberOfStableArrays(int zero, int one, int limit) {
        int MOD = 1000000007;
        int[][][] dp0 = new int[zero + 1][one + 1][limit + 1];
        int[][][] dp1 = new int[zero + 1][one + 1][limit + 1];
        
        if (zero >= 1) dp0[1][0][1] = 1;
        if (one >= 1) dp1[0][1][1] = 1;
        
        for (int z = 0; z <= zero; z++) {
            for (int o = 0; o <= one; o++) {
                for (int run = 1; run <= limit; run++) {
                    if (dp0[z][o][run] > 0) {
                        if (z + 1 <= zero && run + 1 <= limit) {
                            dp0[z + 1][o][run + 1] = (dp0[z + 1][o][run + 1] + dp0[z][o][run]) % MOD;
                        }
                        if (o + 1 <= one) {
                            dp1[z][o + 1][1] = (dp1[z][o + 1][1] + dp0[z][o][run]) % MOD;
                        }
                    }
                    if (dp1[z][o][run] > 0) {
                        if (o + 1 <= one && run + 1 <= limit) {
                            dp1[z][o + 1][run + 1] = (dp1[z][o + 1][run + 1] + dp1[z][o][run]) % MOD;
                        }
                        if (z + 1 <= zero) {
                            dp0[z + 1][o][1] = (dp0[z + 1][o][1] + dp1[z][o][run]) % MOD;
                        }
                    }
                }
            }
        }
        
        long total = 0;
        for (int run = 1; run <= limit; run++) {
            total = (total + dp0[zero][one][run]) % MOD;
            total = (total + dp1[zero][one][run]) % MOD;
        }
        return (int) total;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(zero * one * limit)
space complexity: O(zero * one * limit)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution uses dynamic programming to count valid binary arrays by tracking sequences ending with runs of 0s or 1s. It ensures no run exceeds the limit by transitioning between states. The time and space complexity are cubic in the input sizes, making it efficient for constraints up to 200. The approach is memory-intensive but avoids unnecessary computations.
